title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe | Make Mole Negro
seo.metaDescription: Discover how to make authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) from scratch with our traditional recipe. Rich, complex, and perfect for a Mexican dinner!
longTailKeywords: how to make Oaxacan Black Mole
seo.h1: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) Recipe
h2Headings: Ingredients You Need for Mole Negro
Step-by-Step Instructions for Oaxacan Black Mole
Pro Tips for the Best Mole Negro Results
Serving Suggestions for Your Black Mole
Variations and Substitutions for Mole Negro
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of rich, dark Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served with chicken and rice.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of traditional Oaxacan Black Mole, a culinary masterpiece from Mexico.

description: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary masterpiece, a symphony of flavors from Oaxaca, Mexico. This rich, dark sauce combines chiles, nuts, seeds, spices, and a touch of chocolate, resulting in an incredibly complex and deeply satisfying experience. Perfect for special occasions, it transforms simple chicken or turkey into an unforgettable feast.

instructions.title: Prepare Chiles and Roast Aromatics
instructions.text: First, destem and deseed all the dried chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat and toast the chiles for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant but not burnt. Immediately transfer them to a bowl of hot water and let them rehydrate for at least 15 minutes. Meanwhile, on the same comal, roast the tomatillos, tomatoes, onion pieces, and unpeeled garlic cloves until softened and slightly charred, about 8-10 minutes, turning occasionally. Set aside.

instructions.title: Toast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices
instructions.text: In a clean, dry skillet over medium-low heat, quickly toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant, about 1-2 minutes each. Be careful not to burn them. Remove and set aside. Then, toast the cinnamon stick, whole cloves, black peppercorns, cumin seeds, dried oregano, and thyme together for about 1 minute until aromatic. Set aside.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles. Peel the roasted garlic. In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, roasted tomatillos, tomatoes, onion, peeled garlic, all the toasted nuts, seeds, and spices. Add 1 cup of the hot chicken broth. Blend until a very smooth, thick paste forms. You may need to do this in batches, ensuring a fine consistency. If too thick, add a tiny bit more broth.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Base
instructions.text: Heat the lard or vegetable oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot fat. This step is crucial for flavor development. Stir continuously for about 15 minutes, allowing the paste to darken further and thicken. It should start to pull away from the sides of the pot and develop a rich aroma. Be mindful of splattering.

instructions.title: Simmer and Adjust Seasoning
instructions.text: Once the mole paste has cooked down, gradually stir in the remaining 3 cups of hot chicken broth. Add the Mexican chocolate tablet, salt to taste, and the teaspoon of sugar. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer, then reduce the heat to low and cook, stirring occasionally, for at least 15 minutes to allow the flavors to meld and the sauce to thicken to your desired consistency. The mole should be rich, dark, and glossy.

instructions.title: Serve Your Oaxacan Black Mole
instructions.text: Taste and adjust seasoning one last time; you may need more salt or a touch more sugar to balance the complex flavors. Serve the Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole hot, traditionally over cooked chicken or turkey pieces. Garnish with a sprinkle of sesame seeds and serve alongside Mexican red rice and warm corn tortillas for a complete and unforgettable meal.

tips: For a truly authentic Mole Negro, seek out chilhuacle negro chiles. If unavailable, the combination of ancho, mulato, and pasilla provides a good depth of flavor.
To achieve the smoothest mole, pass the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve after blending. This removes any unblended chile skins or seeds, resulting in a luxurious texture.
Mole Negro freezes beautifully. Make a larger batch and store portions in airtight containers for up to 3 months. Thaw and reheat gently, adding a splash of broth if needed.
article:
Delving into the heart of Mexican cuisine reveals a dish of unparalleled depth and complexity: Mole Negro. Often hailed as the "king of moles," this Oaxacan masterpiece is more than just a sauce; it's a culinary journey, a symphony of flavors, and a testament to centuries of tradition. If you’ve ever dreamed of recreating an authentic Mexican dinner recipe that truly captivates, preparing a rich, smoky, and subtly sweet Oaxacan Black Mole is an experience you won't soon forget.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole?

Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, originates from the vibrant culinary region of Oaxaca, Mexico, a state renowned for its seven distinct moles. Among them, Mole Negro stands out for its intensely dark color, achieved through a meticulous process of charring and roasting an extensive list of ingredients, primarily a variety of dried chiles. It's a dish steeped in history, often reserved for special celebrations, weddings, and holidays, reflecting the care and time invested in its creation.

Unlike simpler sauces, Oaxacan Black Mole boasts a flavor profile that is both profound and intricate. It dances between smoky and sweet, spicy and earthy, with hints of chocolate, nuts, and a medley of aromatic spices. The darkness of the mole is not just visual; it signifies a deep caramelization and transformation of ingredients, resulting in a velvety texture and an unforgettable taste that embodies the soul of Oaxacan cooking. It’s a labor of love, but one that yields an incredibly rewarding and authentic Mexican dinner recipe.

## Ingredients You Need for Mole Negro

The ingredient list for Oaxacan Black Mole might seem daunting at first glance, but each component plays a vital role in building its signature depth and complexity. Aim for quality ingredients, as they will significantly impact the final flavor.

**For the Chiles (the backbone):**
* 6-8 Mulato chiles
* 6-8 Ancho chiles
* 4-6 Pasilla Oaxaca chiles (essential for smoky flavor)
* 4-6 Guajillo chiles
* 2-3 Chipotle chiles (optional, for extra heat and smokiness)

**For the Aromatics & Vegetables:**
* 1 large white onion, roughly chopped
* 6-8 cloves garlic, peeled
* 2 ripe tomatoes, roughly chopped
* 2 tomatillos, husked and quartered
* 1 ripe plantain, sliced
* 1 small tortilla or slice of stale bread

**For the Nuts & Seeds (for thickening and richness):**
* 1/4 cup almonds
* 1/4 cup peanuts
* 2 tablespoons sesame seeds
* 1 tablespoon pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
* 1 tablespoon raisins

**For the Spices (the soul of the mole):**
* 1 cinnamon stick (about 2 inches)
* 4-5 whole cloves
* 1/2 teaspoon whole black peppercorns
* 1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
* 1/4 teaspoon anise seeds
* Pinch of dried thyme, marjoram, and oregano

**For the Sweetener & Chocolate:**
* 1-2 ounces Mexican chocolate (bitter, unsweetened)
* 1 tablespoon piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar

**For Cooking:**
* 1/4 cup lard or vegetable oil
* 6-8 cups chicken or vegetable broth
* Salt to taste

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Oaxacan Black Mole

Making Oaxacan Black Mole is a process that requires patience and attention, but the steps are manageable.

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from all dried chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the chiles in batches for 30 seconds to 1 minute per side, until fragrant and slightly puffed, being careful not to burn them (which would make the mole bitter). Immediately transfer to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft. Drain, reserving some of the soaking liquid.

2.  **Toast Nuts & Spices:** In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, and raisins until fragrant. Remove and set aside. Then, toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, cumin, anise, thyme, marjoram, and oregano until aromatic. Set aside with the nuts.

3.  **Fry Aromatics & Thickeners:** Heat 2 tablespoons of lard or oil in a large pot or Dutch oven. Fry the onion, garlic, tomatoes, and tomatillos until softened and lightly charred. Add the sliced plantain and tortilla/bread, frying until golden. Remove all fried ingredients and set aside.

4.  **Blend Everything:** In batches, combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts and spices, and all the fried aromatics and thickeners in a blender. Add 1-2 cups of broth and a little reserved chile soaking liquid (if needed) to help blend until a very smooth paste forms. You may need to strain the paste through a fine-mesh sieve to achieve the desired silky texture, pressing down firmly to extract all the liquid.

5.  **Cook the Mole Base:** Heat the remaining lard or oil in the same pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole paste. This is a crucial step for developing the deep flavor and color. Cook the paste, stirring constantly, for 30-45 minutes. It will darken significantly, thicken, and release its complex aromas. This process allows the flavors to meld and the raw taste of the chiles to dissipate.

6.  **Simmer and Season:** Gradually stir in the remaining chicken or vegetable broth, Mexican chocolate, and piloncillo/brown sugar. Bring to a gentle simmer. Reduce heat to low, cover partially, and let the Oaxacan Black Mole simmer for at least 1-2 hours, or even longer, stirring occasionally, until it reaches your desired consistency and the flavors have deepened. Season generously with salt to taste. The longer it simmers, the richer and more complex it becomes.

## Pro Tips for the Best Mole Negro Results

Crafting the perfect Oaxacan Black Mole is an art, and these tips will help you achieve an authentic and unforgettable flavor:

*   **Patience is Key:** Don't rush any step, especially the toasting of chiles and the cooking of the mole paste. These processes are fundamental to developing the deep, layered flavors.
*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** Toasting should bring out their fragrance, not burn them. Burnt chiles will impart a bitter taste to your mole that is difficult to fix.
*   **Blend Thoroughly:** A smooth mole is a sign of a well-made mole. Blend in small batches and, if necessary, pass the finished paste through a sieve for a truly velvety texture.
*   **Stir, Stir, Stir:** When cooking the mole paste, constant stirring prevents sticking and burning, ensuring even cooking and flavor development.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole Negro is a balanced dish. Taste frequently and adjust salt, sweetness (with more piloncillo), or even a touch of acidity (with a squeeze of lime) until it’s perfect for your palate.
*   **Make Ahead:** Like many stews and complex sauces, Oaxacan Black Mole often tastes even better the next day, after the flavors have had more time to meld and deepen. It freezes beautifully too!

## Serving Suggestions for Your Black Mole

Once your magnificent Oaxacan Black Mole is ready, the serving possibilities are endless, making it a versatile Mexican dinner recipe.

*   **Classic Pairing:** Traditionally, Mole Negro is served with poached or roasted chicken or turkey. Shredded chicken bathed in the rich mole is a truly divine experience.
*   **Other Meats:** It also pairs wonderfully with pork shoulder, beef short ribs, or even duck.
*   **Vegetarian Delights:** For a vegetarian option, serve it over roasted sweet potatoes, grilled portobello mushrooms, or as a sauce for cheese enchiladas or tamales.
*   **Simple Sides:** Always serve with warm corn tortillas for scooping up every last drop, and a side of fluffy white rice to soak up the luxurious sauce.
*   **Garnish:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, a few sprigs of fresh cilantro, or thinly sliced red onion adds both visual appeal and a delightful textural contrast.

## Variations and Substitutions for Mole Negro

While the classic Oaxacan Black Mole is a masterpiece, you can explore minor variations to suit your taste or available ingredients.

*   **Chile Adjustments:** If Pasilla Oaxaca chiles are hard to find, you can try substituting with more Mulato or Ancho chiles, though you might lose some of the signature smokiness. For more heat, increase the Chipotle chiles or add a small amount of árbol chiles.
*   **Nut & Seed Choices:** While almonds and peanuts are traditional, feel free to experiment with other nuts like pecans or cashews, or seeds like sunflower seeds, to slightly alter the richness and flavor profile.
*   **Sweetener Alternatives:** If piloncillo isn't available, dark brown sugar is the closest substitute. Dates or prunes can also be added for a natural sweetness and depth.
*   **Thickeners:** Instead of a tortilla, you can use a slice of stale bread or even a few animal crackers for thickening and a subtle sweetness.
*   **Vegetarian Mole:** For a fully plant-based version, ensure you use vegetable broth and a plant-based oil instead of lard. The richness of the nuts and seeds will still provide plenty of body.

Creating Oaxacan Black Mole is an undertaking that rewards you with an incredibly rich, complex, and utterly satisfying dish. It’s a culinary adventure that connects you to the heart of Mexican tradition and leaves a lasting impression on anyone who tastes it. Embrace the process, savor the aromas, and enjoy the profound flavors of this iconic Mexican dinner recipe.
